cards.definition: A public figure who represents and embodies a brand's values in public communications. Gucci replaced heritage ambassadors with contemporary figures like Beyoncé and Harry Styles to signal repositioning toward younger consumers.
The weakening of a brand's equity or premium positioning caused by over-extension of the product line, inappropriate distribution, or associations inconsistent with core brand identity.
The commercial and perceptual value added to a product by its brand name, associations, and consumer loyalty — enabling premium pricing, preference, and advocacy beyond functional attributes alone.

A strategic process of restoring a brand's relevance, desirability, and commercial performance after decline, typically involving changes to creative direction, target consumer, product range, and communications.

Gucci's ten-year strategic charter embedding environmental sustainability, employee welfare, community engagement, and production efficiency as core organisational commitments, tracked publicly via the Gucci Equilibrium platform.
A go-to-market model in which a brand sells directly to end consumers through its own stores rather than wholesale intermediaries, giving control over pricing, experience, data, and margin.

Clothing and accessories designed to be worn across the gender spectrum without masculine or feminine coding. Gucci normalised this under Alessandro Michele, aligning with Gen Z and millennial identity trends.

An online platform launched in 2018 that publicly tracks Gucci's real-time progress against its sustainability commitments, functioning as a transparency mechanism and authenticity signal for socially conscious consumers.

The French luxury conglomerate formerly known as PPR that owns Gucci, Saint Laurent, Balenciaga, and others. In 2019 it operated in 120+ countries with €15.8B revenue; Gucci contributed 63% of revenues and 78% of profits.

The expansion of an existing brand into a new product category adjacent to its core offering. The strategic risk is that categories with different price points or positioning may dilute the parent brand's equity.

An integrated retailing approach providing consumers a seamless brand experience across all purchase channels — physical stores, branded e-commerce, third-party online, social media, and mobile — with consistent product, pricing, and service.

The luxury market segment comprising fashion, leather goods, shoes, jewellery, watches, and fragrances and beauty products that are personally consumed. This segment was worth €281 billion globally in 2019.
A brand strategy positioning a brand around a higher social, environmental, or cultural mission beyond product attributes, resonating with consumers who expect brands to address societal challenges.

The resale and repurchase of pre-owned luxury goods via platforms such as TheRealReal and Farfetch. The second-hand luxury market reached €26B in 2019, growing four times faster than the primary market.

Gucci's internal advisory body of its most talented employees under 30, created by CEO Marco Bizzarri to provide generational consumer insights to senior leadership and institutionalise millennial perspective-taking.

A merchandising framework dividing a brand's assortment into stable core products (Gucci: 70%) and trend-driven items (Gucci: 30%). Staples provide revenue stability; trendy items generate media coverage and new consumer acquisition.
